preFix.title: Joining plastic soil pipe with solvent weld
preFix.subtitle: Make permanent push-in joints on 110 mm PVC soil pipe using solvent cement
preFix.excerpt: How to cut, prepare, and solvent-weld a 110 mm PVC soil pipe joint, including squaring the cut end, cleaning and priming both surfaces, applying solvent cement, making the joint within the working time, and supporting the joint while it sets.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Marley Plumbing + Drainage installation guide. CIPHE good practice guidance on soil stack connections.

content.text: A
solvent-welded
soil pipe joint is permanent: once the solvent has set, the joint cannot be undone without cutting. Everything must be prepared before the cement is applied: the
pipe end square
, both surfaces clean and dry, the
socket depth
marked on the pipe, and the fitting positioned to accept the pipe in the correct orientation. Work in a ventilated space: the solvent vapour is strong.
Step 1, cut the pipe square

Mark the cut position on the pipe and wrap a strip of masking tape around the pipe at the cut position, using the tape edge as a cutting guide. The tape edge produces a more accurate square line than a pencil mark alone. Cut through the pipe with the panel saw, keeping the cut on the waste side of the line. Deburr the cut end inside and outside with the utility knife.
Step 2, mark the insertion depth

Insert the pipe end into the socket dry and push until it meets the socket stop. Mark the pipe at the socket face: this is the insertion depth line. Remove the pipe and measure the insertion depth from the end of the pipe to this line. Both the pipe end and the socket must be cleaned and primed before applying the solvent cement.
Step 3, clean both surfaces

Wipe the inside of the fitting socket and the outside of the pipe end (to the depth mark) with the manufacturer's cleaning fluid on a clean cloth. This removes mould-release agent, grease, and surface contamination. Allow to dry: 30 seconds in normal conditions. Do not touch the cleaned surfaces with bare hands after cleaning.
Step 4, apply cement and assemble
Apply a thin, even coat of
solvent cement

to the inside of the socket and immediately a second coat to the outside of the pipe end, working within the socket depth mark. Push the pipe into the socket immediately with a slight twisting motion (30°) and push firmly to the full depth stop. Hold the joint under hand pressure for 30 seconds: do not allow the pipe to spring back. Wipe off any surplus cement that squeezes out of the joint with a dry cloth before it sets.
Step 5, support the joint and allow to cure

Do not allow the joint to carry any weight or be subjected to movement for at least 5 minutes. Support the pipe either side of the joint with pipe clips during the cure period. Full chemical cure takes 24 hours: do not test with water for at least 1 hour. In cold conditions (below 10°C), extend the cure time before testing.
Variations

Ring-seal fittings are the alternative to solvent-weld for soil pipe. Push the plain pipe end into the socket to compress a rubber ring: no adhesive is needed. Ring-seal joints allow for thermal expansion and are easier to assemble. They can be pulled apart by mechanical force, which solvent-welded joints cannot. In a soil stack, use ring-seal fittings at the top of long vertical runs for thermal movement. Branch connections can be solvent-welded.

attrs.fix: Cut out the leaking joint — it cannot be repaired. Clean both surfaces and remake the joint with attention to full insertion depth and clean surfaces.
attrs.cause: Pipe not pushed to full socket depth; cement applied unevenly; surfaces contaminated when cement was applied.
attrs.symptom: Solvent-welded joint leaks when water is flowing

attrs.fix: Cut out and replace. There is no way to rotate or adjust a solvent-welded joint. Always dry-fit and confirm orientation before opening the cement tin.
attrs.cause: Rotation not checked before cementing; pipe twisted during the push but not aligned to the correct position before holding.
attrs.symptom: Fitting is in the wrong orientation after the cement sets

attrs.fix: Use the correct solvent cement for the pipe material — check the packaging. Discard any cement that has thickened or gelled; it will not form an adequate joint.
attrs.cause: Wrong solvent cement used — grey PVC solvent for a brown ABS pipe (or vice versa); cement too old or dried out.
attrs.symptom: Joint has poor adhesion and separates easily

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
